extra.message: User input from request.POST['first_name'] is base64-decoded and passed directly to exec() with no sanitization. This allows an attacker to execute arbitrary Python code on the server (Remote Code Execution).

User-controlled filename from request.GET['p'] is concatenated with a base path via os.path.join() and used to open and read arbitrary files from the filesystem. An attacker can read sensitive files like /etc/passwd or settings.py using path traversal sequences.

User-supplied query string 'qs' from request.GET is passed to the template and rendered with the |safe filter in form.html:28 (value="{{qs|safe}}"), disabling Django's auto-escaping. This allows reflected XSS attacks where an attacker injects arbitrary HTML/JavaScript via the qs parameter.

User-controlled URL path segment 'path' is passed to the template and rendered with the |safe filter in path.html:49 (var myvar = "{{path|safe}}"), disabling Django's auto-escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by crafting a malicious URL path.

User-supplied query string 'qs' from request.GET is passed to the template and rendered with the |safe filter in query.html:37 (var myvar = "{{qs|safe}}"), disabling Django's auto-escaping. This allows reflected XSS attacks by injecting malicious JavaScript via the qs parameter.

User-supplied query string 'qs' from request.GET is passed to the template and rendered with the |safe filter in csrf/image.html:48 (var x = "{{qs|safe}}"), disabling Django's auto-escaping. This allows reflected XSS enabling CSRF-style attacks via injected image tags.

User-controlled URL from request.GET['url'] is passed directly to django.shortcuts.redirect() with no validation or whitelisting. An attacker can craft a URL that redirects users to arbitrary external malicious sites (phishing, malware distribution).

User-controlled parameter 'fwd' from request.GET is used to look up and call any function in globals(). An attacker can access the admin page or any other function by supplying ?fwd=admin, bypassing all access controls. This effectively allows unauthorized invocation of any globally-defined view function.

Admin access is granted solely based on request.GET['action'] == 'admin' with no authentication, session validation, or authorization checks. Any unauthenticated user can access the admin interface by appending ?action=admin to the URL.

The dor_user_profile view retrieves and allows modification of any user's profile based solely on the 'userid' URL parameter without verifying the requesting user owns that profile. An attacker can access or modify any other user's data (including admin users) by changing the userid parameter (e.g., /users/2 to access the admin's profile).

A hardcoded Django SECRET_KEY ('h++jszpm)i@p%ay_b=cp#()^od!qns14)h%@qm3)p=cuo+st^a') is committed in source code. This key is used for cryptographic signing of sessions, CSRF tokens, and password reset tokens. Exposure allows attackers to forge session cookies and CSRF tokens.

Django's CsrfViewMiddleware is commented out in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES, disabling CSRF protection for the entire application. Combined with @csrf_exempt at views.py:164, all POST endpoints are vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks, allowing attackers to perform state-changing actions on behalf of authenticated users.

DEBUG and TEMPLATE_DEBUG are set to True (production-intended setting). When combined with unhandled exceptions (e.g., the 'boom' view at views.py:144), this exposes full stack traces, internal file paths, local variables, and application internals to end users, aiding attackers in reconnaissance and exploit development.

The Django Debug Toolbar's SHOW_TOOLBAR_CALLBACK enables the toolbar when any GET parameter, POST parameter, or cookie named 'debug' is present. Any unauthenticated user can append ?debug=1 to any URL to view detailed request information, SQL queries, template context, and other sensitive internals.

The 'boom' view deliberately raises an unhandled Exception. With DEBUG=True in settings, this will produce a detailed Django error page exposing stack traces, file paths, local variables, and configuration details to any unauthenticated user visiting the /misconfiguration/boom endpoint.

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through an Error Message
